original_abstract: While Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have achieved remarkable success in single-label image classification, adapting them for multi-label imagery remains a significant challenge. This difficulty stems primarily from the complexity of varied object layouts and a lack of sufficiently annotated multi-label training data. In this paper, we introduce Hypotheses-CNN-Pooling (HCP), a flexible deep learning architecture designed to overcome these hurdles. The HCP framework processes an arbitrary number of object segment hypotheses through a shared CNN; the resulting outputs are then consolidated via max pooling to generate final multi-label predictions. Key advantages of the HCP architecture include its ability to train without ground-truth bounding box annotations and its inherent robustness to noisy or redundant hypotheses. Furthermore, the shared CNN component is highly adaptable and can be effectively pre-trained on massive single-label datasets like ImageNet. Our experimental evaluations on the Pascal VOC 2007 and VOC 2012 benchmarks demonstrate that HCP significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art methods. Notably, HCP achieves a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 90.5% on VOC 2012, which increases to 93.2% when fused with complementary hand-crafted features.
humanized_abstract: Although Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have been very effective for single-label image classification, multi-label image classification is still a big challenge. One of the most compelling reasons is the complexity of different possible object layouts and lack of the sufficient amount of training data in most multi-label image classification benchmarks. In this paper, we propose Hypotheses-CNN-Pooling (HCP) architecture, a new deep learning approach to accomplish multi-label image classification. Instead of having a fixed number of bounding box regions as in typical CNN, an arbitrary number of object segment hypotheses are input to a shared CNN. Their corresponding outputs are then given to max-pooling operation, in order to obtain the final multi-label classification results. Our network, HCP, is showed to be able to learn the multi-label image classification task from the images and their segment hypotheses without relying to the ground-truth bounding box locations. It is also robust against the noisy or redundant segment hypotheses in an image. The shared CNN part in our network is also easy to be optimized and can fully take the advantage of the pre-trained ImageNet model. We conduct experimental comparisons on two popular benchmarks – the Pascal VOC 2007 and VOC 2012. Our experimental results show that our proposed deep neural network, HCP, outperforms the state-of-the-arts on these two benchmarks; by achieving 90.5% mean Average Precision (mAP) on VOC 2012 while improves to 93.2% when we combine our proposed network, HCP, with the global descriptors of hand-crafted features.
